mnemonics: SPACE-Time Tradeoff: Sometimes Paying A Cost Early (saves) Time Later
keyPoints: You can often reduce an algorithm's time complexity by using more memory (space) to store precomputed results.
Conversely, you can often reduce an algorithm's space complexity by re-computing results on the fly, which increases time complexity.
This is a fundamental design principle where you trade one resource (space) for another (time) based on the constraints of your problem.
commonMistakes: Assuming a space-time tradeoff always exists for every problem; some problems are inherently time or space intensive.
Over-optimizing for space when time is the critical constraint (or vice-versa) without analyzing the system's requirements.
Implementing a complex space-time tradeoff (like a dynamic programming table) for a problem size so small that a brute-force solution would be more efficient.
